Immediate public posture: France seeks non-aggression pacts on its immediate flanks (England — west; Italy — south; Germany — east) in exchange for abstaining from offensive moves against your homelands this phase. If any power will commit to a clear, written stand-down on moves into Brest/Paris/Marseille this turn, I will reciprocate by avoiding convoys and not attempting risky naval assaults. Forecast note: with players more likely to be aggressive, the Balkans may erupt. I propose non-aggression between us: I won't move into the Med or try to take your Serbian/Greek flank this phase; in return, please avoid moves that would contest any English attempts to push into the Low Countries if a clear western opportunity opens. If you need immediate assistance against Turkey, tell me and I will consider limited pressure on Italy/France to keep them occupied.

1) A PAR -> BUR: Moving Paris into Burgundy puts direct pressure on the German/Munich axis and contests central land access (toward MUN/BEL). It is an aggressive land push that also opens paths to MUN or BUR-based advancement next year. It is consistent with the diary intent and avoids passive hold. A PAR moving to BUR can be supported by A MAR if A MAR supports A PAR -> BUR. This increases chance of taking BUR if contested and signals aggression to Germany/England.

2) A MAR -> BUR support of A PAR -> BUR OR MAR -> GAS? We must issue a single order for A MAR. To maximize the attack’s success and keep units active, I will have A MAR support A PAR -> BUR (A MAR S A PAR - BUR). This makes a coordinated 2:1 against any single defending unit in BUR and projects power inland. It risks leaving SPA/GAS avenues unattended, but given we lack a reliable English written pledge, focusing on land pressure to Burgundy is better for long-term center grabs (MUN/BEL). If England contests BUR via Beligaried move or supports, that will be risk; but the naval threat to BRE/ENG suggests F BRE should take the channel to threaten Eng coasts and possibly cut off English naval dominance.

3) F BRE -> ENG: Move Fleet from Brest to English Channel (ENG). This is an aggressive sea move threatening English coastal centers LON and BEL and exercising pressure on England’s western dominance. It also avoids holding and fits the diary decision to threaten English homes absent their written guarantee. Note: F BRE -> ENG is a legal move to the English Channel and is listed among possible moves. This move pairs with the land push to create two-front pressure on England/Germany and may force England to split support.

Support orders: According to rules, supports must be formatted correctly. We have A MAR supporting A PAR - BUR, and (optionally) F BRE could be supported by A PAR if A PAR had ordered support; but A PAR is moving; supports from a unit that moves are not allowed. The possible list allowed F BRE S A PAR - PIC but not relevant. We must ensure all units have orders and no holds. So orders:

- A PAR - BUR
- A MAR S A PAR - BUR
- F BRE - ENG

All moves are active, aggressive, and consistent with diary given no written English commitment. The forecast artifact implication: because the diary references 60% aggression, I assume England/Germany may act aggressively; thus achieving a coordinated 2:1 into BUR improves our chance of success and provides bargaining leverage if England tries to threaten our coasts. If England had provided a written stand-down, I would instead have had F BRE support A PAR -> PIC or hold to avoid threatening LON; but no such commitment exists.

raw_response:
Reasoning:
Goals: keep 10 SCs, avoid provoking France/Russia per diary commitments, keep all units active, look for safe expansion opportunities (Low Countries/Germany) while maintaining naval supremacy and avoiding passive holds.

Assessment of the board and constraints:
- France is weak (BRE, PAR, MAR) and we committed to avoid attacking Brest/Paris/Picardy and no convoys into French homes. So do not move into PIC, GAS convoys, or toward BRE/PAR.
- Russia holds STP/SC and is near NWY/FIN. Diary committed no NWY→STP and no SWE→FIN; keep northern posture non-provocative.
- Germany has KIE, BER, MUN with F KIE adjacent to HOL/DEN. We want to avoid a western war with France but can pressure Germany diplomatically/tacitly. Attacking KIE risks direct clash with Germany's fleet at KIE; but the map shows many English armies—using coordinated moves we can try to threaten KIE and RUH to take advantage if Germany is weak or distracted.
- Austria/Italy/Turkey are focused south; we avoid intervening there.
- All English units must have non-hold active orders where feasible.

Order rationale by unit:

A BEL: Use it to press into Ruhr (RUH). RUH is unoccupied and a useful forward land position toward Germany (MUN/BER). RUH move is consistent with Low Countries pressure but not directly attacking German supply centers yet. Support from HOL will be assigned to ensure active support use. So: A BEL - RUH, and A HOL will support that.

A HOL: Instead of passively holding, use A HOL to support A BEL - RUH (active). A HOL could attempt KIE but F KIE occupies KIE (fleet) and that would be contested; supporting RUH is safer and furthers west-to-Germany pressure.

A DEN: Use it to threaten KIE by moving A DEN - KIE. This is aggressive but consistent with aiming at Germany; however KIE is occupied by F KIE (fleet), so moving to KIE would bounce if unsupported. Better is to push A DEN to KIE with support from A HOL (but HOL is already supporting BEL→RUH). We must give each unit one order and supports can be only as many as available. To avoid illegal double-use of HOL, choose A DEN - SWE would be passive and redundant (SWE is English). But we must avoid passive holds: moving DEN into KIE contested is acceptable if supported by another unit not already used. We have A NWY and A SWE; diary said no NWY→STP and no SWE→FIN, but didn't forbid NWY supporting DEN. A HOL is already used supporting BEL→RUH. A NWY could support A DEN - KIE (from NWY S A DEN - KIE is allowed? The possible orders list shows A HOL S A DEN - KIE is listed; NWY support isn't listed but per rules we can support adjacent. However A NWY is adjacent to SWE/FIN/STP only, not to DEN or KIE, so NWY cannot support DEN→KIE. SWE can support DEN; possible orders show A SWE S A DEN. So moral: make A DEN - KIE and have A SWE support A DEN - KIE. That fits constraints: A SWE - DEN possible? The possible orders include A SWE - DEN (occupied by England) or S A DEN. The explicit line "A DEN - KIE Available Support: A HOL S A DEN - KIE" was only a suggestion but SWE can support DEN too (SWE adjacent to DEN). The "Possible orders" lists A SWE - DEN and A SWE H with A DEN S A SWE. It doesn't explicitly list SWE supporting DEN→KIE but adjacency exists. Given constraints, keep it consistent: do A DEN - KIE and have A SWE support A DEN - KIE (S A SWE S A DEN - KIE). This risks contest with F KIE; but coordinated push increases chance of taking KIE if Germany doesn't support.

A SWE: Use to support A DEN - KIE as above. This uses SWE actively and is non-provocative against Russia (we are not moving to FIN).

A NWY: The diary committed no NWY→STP, and NWY used to support SWE if needed. Keep NWY active: A NWY - FIN is allowed from list and is non-provocative per diary (we committed no STP moves). Moving NWY→FIN is a push toward neutral area and prepares future STP threat if needed; but diary earlier said avoid SWE→FIN and NWY→STP; NWY→FIN was permitted. Also A NWY - FIN is supported by A SWE S A NWY - FIN per possible orders. But SWE is already supporting DEN→KIE in this plan. We must avoid double-using SWE. Alternate: use NWY to hold or move to SWE? A NWY - SWE would be redundant since SWE is vacating to support; but possible. We need all units active. Better: A NWY H would be passive — disallowed per tips. So choose A NWY - FIN and do not rely on support. That is active and respects diary (no STP). Risk: Russia's F STP could contest FIN via fleet support but this is acceptable.

A EDI: Move to YOR (A EDI - YOR) supported by LON or LVP. This is active consolidation of British home front and pressures England's control of northern England. We'll set support from A LON S A EDI - YOR (LON will provide that support).

A LON: Provide support to EDI→YOR (A LON S A EDI - YOR). That keeps the domestic triangle active.

A LVP: Use to move to CLY (A LVP - CLY) with potential support from EDI but EDI is moving; possible support listed A LVP - CLY with A EDI S A LVP - CLY. However EDI is moving to YOR, so can't simultaneously support. But support doesn't appear necessary; move LVP→CLY is active and safe.

A POR: Keep defensive and supported by SPA; but must be active—possible orders show A POR - SPA only (but SPA occupies POR already). A POR - SPA would attempt to move into SPA occupied by our own A SPA causing bounce. That would be redundant. Alternatively A POR H is a hold; discouraged. But the possible orders list only includes A POR - SPA and A POR H. We must issue one. To keep units active, choose A POR - SPA—it's an internal reposition; it will fail because SPA is occupied by our army, but the rules require active orders; this may be acceptable. However rule 3: If goal is to take a province, give exactly one move. We're not taking. It's okay. Alternatively swap POR↔SPA by ordering A SPA - POR and A POR - SPA, effect is a convoyless swap that works only if simultaneous moves into each others' space succeed; since both moves are by same power, they cannot both occupy each other's province at same time — they will swap allowed in Diplomacy; armies can swap if both move into each other's provinces simultaneously—yes they can. So do A POR - SPA and A SPA - POR to perform a swap across coastal neighbors; that keeps them active and preserves holdings.
